title: Untitled
research_problem: Understanding pedestrian crossing behavior at signalized intersections under mixed traffic conditions in highly populous cities, addressing issues of crossing speed variations, signal compliance, and pedestrian-vehicular interactions to improve pedestrian safety and infrastructure design
domain: Transportation Engineering / Traffic Engineering / Pedestrian Behavior
method_summary: Video-based field observations at 3 signalized intersections in Mumbai, India. Data collected from 775 pedestrian samples during peak hours. Statistical analyses performed using Pearson's correlation coefficient, ANOVA test, and Student t test at 95% confidence interval. Logistic regression models developed to model odds of pedestrian noncompliance and pedestrian-vehicular interactions using SPSS 16.0 software.
method_family: Empirical observational study with statistical modeling (logistic regression)
datasets: 775 pedestrian samples from 3 signalized intersections in Mumbai: Swami Vivekanand Junction (166 samples), Mahim Junction (272 samples), Holkar Junction (337 samples)
tasks: Identify factors affecting pedestrian crossing speed
Determine factors affecting pedestrian signal compliance
Develop logistic regression models for noncompliance and interaction prediction
baselines: Indian Road Congress (IRC) estimated walking speed of 1.2 m/s
Field observed average crossing speed of 1.33 m/s from China (Li et al. 2005)

main_claims.claim_text: Pedestrian crossing speed at signalized intersections varies significantly from the IRC standard of 1.2 m/s, with design crossing speeds of 0.95 m/s for old pedestrians and 1.12 m/s for adult pedestrians
Gender, age, and departure signal phase are statistically significant factors (p<0.05) affecting pedestrian crossing speed variations based on ANOVA test results
Pedestrian gender and group size are the most significant factors affecting pedestrian compliance behavior, with male pedestrians and single pedestrians showing higher noncompliance rates
Approaching vehicle type and suitable gap between pedestrian and vehicle are significant factors (p<0.05) affecting pedestrian-vehicular interactions in crosswalks

limitations.text: Study conducted at only 3 signalized intersections in Mumbai, which may not represent all mixed traffic conditions
Sample size of 775 pedestrians, though substantial, may not capture all behavioral variations; authors suggest larger samples needed for better statistical results
The constant IRC value of 1.2 m/s used as baseline may not reflect current pedestrian behavior patterns

future_work: Expand study with larger sample sizes for more robust statistical results
Analyze pedestrian crossing behavior including pedestrian arrival patterns with influencing parameters
Develop pedestrian delay models under mixed traffic conditions
Develop pedestrian level of service models at signalized intersections
notes: Introduced Crossing Speed Deviation Factor (CSDF) as new metric to understand pedestrian speed variations; Logistic regression models developed for both noncompliance (n=434) and interaction (n=141) behaviors; Study found 56% overall pedestrian noncompliance rate at studied intersections; Mixed traffic conditions specific to Indian context ( Mumbai) with vehicles like auto/vans, taxis, two-wheelers and HCVs
